title: practical english usage - Part 286

with meaning and grammar. Some other words are more conveniently explained This Section deals with a number of words which  often cause problems to do INTRODUCTION AtoZ Section 31   Word  Problems from vocabulary •  352  about and on Electricity cables stretch over/across the fields.

The plane was flying over/across Denmark.

We   use over or across (►   357) when one thing crosses another.

He put on a coat over his pyjamas.

There is cloud over the South of England.

We   prefer over when one thing covers and/or touches another.

We've got a little house above the lake. (     ... ever the lake.) We   use above when one thing is not directly over another.

above 'not  directly     over': Can you see the helicopter above/over the palace?

The water came up above/over our knees.

with this meaning.

Above and over can both mean 'higher than’ . Above is more common above          over 1  'higher     than':       or 354 above       and   over ►    376 about      and   around I'm not about to pay 100 dollars for that dress.

Not about to can mean 'unwilling to’ .

I was about to go to bed when the telephone rang.

Don't go out now - we're about to have lunch.

About + infinitive (with to) means 'going to very soon’ ; 'just going to’ .

On  suggests that a book, talk, etc is more serious, suitable for specialists.

We   use about to talk about ordinary, more general kinds of communication.

a lecture on economics - a conversation about money a textbook on African history - ɑ  book for children about Africa and its peoples Compare: 352 about       and   on vocabulary •  357  across, over and through See if you can jump  across/over the stream.                -> His village is just across/over the border.

river, road, bridge, etc'.

Across and over can both be used to mean 'on or to the other side of a line, across            over 1 on/to    the   other     side    of  (a  line):      and 357 acro    ss,   over     and   through        ______________ not In my opinion, Anna's boyfriend is an idiot, (     Aeeerding-te-mer- . .) According to Anna, her boyfriend is brilliant. (= If what Anna says is true,...) We   do not usually give our own opinions with according to. Compare: The train gets in at 8.27, according to the timetable.

According to Harry, it's a good film.

According to X means ‘ in X's opinion', ‘ if what X  says is true'.

356 acc   ording       to                               __ I agreed to meet them here. (More normal than I accepted to meet.. .) Before an infinitive, we  usually use agree, not accept.

355 accept       and   agree For other meanings of these words, see a good dictionary.

The  difference between below and under is similar. ►    399 There are cheap flights at weekends: see over.

See over means ‘ look on the next page'.

Our  village is just above Cardiff on the map.

For prices and delivery charges, see above.

The above rules and regulations apply to all students.

In a book or paper, above means ‘ earlier on the page' or ‘ on an earlier page’ .

6 books     and   papers There were over 100,000 people at the festival.

The police said she was driving at over llθmph.

You have to be over 18 to see this film.

‘ more than'.

We usually use over, not above, to talk about ages and speeds, and to mean over 5 ages,    speeds,     'more    than': She's well above average in intelligence.

The summit  of Everest is about 8000 metres above sea level.

The temperature is three degrees above zero.

where we  think of a vertical scale.

Above is used in measurements of temperature and height, and in other cases above 4 measurements: Word  Problems from A  to Z      Section 31 vocabulary •  358  actual(ly) Actually is more common in British than American English.

’ Hello, Jack. Nice to see you.’ ’Actually, my name's Andy.' The book says she died aged 47, but her actual age was 43.
